---
title: "Quiz: scalar types and ranges"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

A short quiz on Rust's scalar types — what each one stores, how much it can hold, and which one to reach for in common bioinformatics situations. Guess before peeking.

12 questions, easy to trickier. The key idea behind every question: **the type you pick decides the range you can store**, and an overflow either panics (in debug builds) or silently wraps (in release builds), so picking too small is a real bug.

---

## 1. Type of an integer literal

```rust
let x = 42;
```

What type does the compiler infer for `x`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
When you don't tell the compiler, Rust falls back on a default integer type. It's the same default NumPy and many other languages use.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**[`i32`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.i32.html)** — Rust's default integer type when nothing in the context forces a different one. To pick a different type, write a suffix (`42_u8`, `42_i64`) or an annotation (`let x: u32 = 42;`).
:::

---

## 2. `'A'` vs `b'A'`

```rust
let a = 'A';
let b = b'A';
```

What types are `a` and `b`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Single quotes give you one character. Add the `b` prefix and you get the *byte* representation of that character. The two types are different sizes.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
- `a` is [`char`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.char.html) — a 4-byte Unicode scalar value.
- `b` is [`u8`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u8.html) — an unsigned 8-bit byte. Numerically it equals 65 (the ASCII code for `'A'`).

For DNA (A, C, G, T are pure ASCII) we almost always store bases as `u8`. `char` would waste 3 bytes per base.
:::

---

## 3. Maximum value of `u8`

What is the largest value a `u8` can hold?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
A `u8` is an *unsigned* 8-bit integer. 8 bits give you 2⁸ distinct values.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**255** (which is `2⁸ − 1`). A `u8` holds the values `0..=255`. That happens to be exactly enough to store one ASCII byte — and exactly enough to store a Phred quality score (Phred caps at Q93 in practice).
:::

---

## 4. Range of `i8`

What is the *range* of `i8`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
A signed 8-bit integer uses one bit for the sign. The negative side has one more representable value than the positive side (two's-complement encoding).
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**`-128..=127`** (`−2⁷` to `2⁷ − 1`). In Rust you can write [`i8::MIN`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.i8.html#associatedconstant.MIN) and [`i8::MAX`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.i8.html#associatedconstant.MAX) — these constants exist for every integer type and are the safe way to reason about a type's bounds.
:::

---

## 5. Right type for a Phred quality score

A FASTQ quality character is an ASCII byte; subtracting 33 gives the Phred score (0..93 in real data).

Which type should you use to store the score?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
The values are 0..93 — well within the range of one specific small type.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**`u8`.** Phred scores are non-negative and never exceed ~93, which fits in `u8` (`0..=255`) with room to spare. Using `i8` would work but wastes the negative half; using `i32` would work but waste 3 of the 4 bytes per score on a million-read FASTQ.
:::

---

## 6. Right type for a position on human chromosome 1

Human chr1 is about **250 million** base pairs long.

Which unsigned integer type is the smallest one that holds any position?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
`u16` maxes at 65 535 (~65k). `u32` maxes around 4.3 billion. Which fits 250 million?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**`u32`** (maximum 4 294 967 295). `u16` is far too small. `u64` works but doubles the memory footprint of every position. For a BAM-sized table of read alignments, that doubling matters.

For the *whole human genome* in one coordinate system (~3 billion bp) `u32` still works, but you're getting close to the limit. Most bioinformatics tools use `u32` for per-chromosome positions and `i64`/`u64` for global indexing into archives.
:::

---

## 7. Default float type

```rust
let g = 0.42;
```

What is the type of `g`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Rust's default float type is the one that gets you the most precision — useful for accumulating things like GC fractions across millions of reads without rounding error compounding.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**[`f64`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.f64.html)** — 64-bit IEEE-754 double, ~15-17 significant decimal digits. To pick `f32` instead (smaller but less precise) you write a suffix: `0.42_f32`. ML / GPU code is the main place where `f32` is the default — half the memory, faster matrix multiplies, precision usually adequate.
:::

---

## 8. Integer vs float division

```rust
let a = 5 / 2;
let b = 5.0 / 2.0;
```

What are `a` and `b`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
The two operands' types decide what operation the compiler runs. Integer arithmetic and float arithmetic give different results for non-divisible numerators.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
- `a` is **`2`** (`i32`) — integer division, truncates toward zero.
- `b` is **`2.5`** (`f64`) — float division.

This is a common source of off-by-one bugs in GC-content code. Write `(gc_count as f64) / (total as f64)` to get a true fraction, never `gc_count / total`.
:::

---

## 9. `255u8 + 1` — what happens?

```rust
let x: u8 = 255;
let y = x + 1;
```

What does this code do?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
255 is the maximum `u8`. The behaviour differs between `cargo run` (debug build) and `cargo run --release` — and that difference is deliberate.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
- **Debug build** (`cargo run`, `cargo test`): **panics** with an "attempt to add with overflow" message.
- **Release build** (`cargo run --release`): **wraps to 0** silently.

If you actually *want* wraparound, use [`x.wrapping_add(1)`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u8.html#method.wrapping_add). If you want to fail gracefully, use [`x.checked_add(1)`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u8.html#method.checked_add) which returns `Option<u8>` (`None` on overflow).
:::

---

## 10. `'A' as u8`

```rust
let b: u8 = 'A' as u8;
```

What is the value of `b`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
The `as` cast on a `char` gives you its Unicode code point as a number. For an ASCII character, that's just the ASCII value.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**`65`** — the ASCII code for `'A'`. Going the other way, `b'A'` is itself the literal `65_u8`. Converting a `char` to `u8` with `as` only succeeds cleanly for ASCII; for non-ASCII it silently truncates the Unicode code point to the low 8 bits — almost never what you want for sequence data.
:::

---

## 11. Type of `s.len()`

```rust
let s: &str = "ACGT";
let n = s.len();
```

What is the type of `n`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Lengths and indices in Rust use a special type whose width depends on the machine — 64-bit on most laptops, 32-bit on embedded targets.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**[`usize`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.usize.html)** — "pointer-sized unsigned integer". On a 64-bit machine that's `u64`; on a 32-bit machine it's `u32`. Every length, capacity, slice index, and `Vec` size in Rust uses `usize`. To use one as an `f64` for an arithmetic ratio: `n as f64`.

(Aside: `s.len()` here is **4 — the number of UTF-8 bytes**, not the number of `char`s. For an ASCII string like `"ACGT"` those numbers agree; for `"αβγ"` they don't.)
:::

---

## 12. Mutability

```rust
let x = 5;
x = 6;
```

Does this compile?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Rust bindings are immutable by default. You have to opt in to mutation with one keyword.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**No** — compile error: *"cannot assign twice to immutable variable `x`"*. To allow reassignment you write `let mut x = 5;`. The default is immutable so the compiler can catch accidental rebinding — and so a reader can trust that an unmutated-looking binding really doesn't change.

(Subtlety: Rust also allows *shadowing* — `let x = 5; let x = 6;` *does* compile, because the second `let` introduces a new binding with the same name. That is different from mutation.)
:::

---

## Where this leads

Four numbers to keep in your head:

| Type | Max value | Use for |
|---|---|---|
| `u8` | 255 | DNA bases (as bytes), Phred scores, anything ≤ 255 |
| `u32` | ~4.3 × 10⁹ | per-chromosome positions, k-mer counts in a single sample |
| `u64` | ~1.8 × 10¹⁹ | global file offsets, read indices in giant FASTQs |
| `f64` | — | any fractional quantity (GC fraction, p-value, score) |

Pick the smallest type that comfortably holds your data, then reach for the helper methods (`checked_add`, `saturating_sub`, `wrapping_mul`) when you specifically need overflow control.
